news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in micro-scale OLED innovation for smart glasses, advanced genome modeling spanning all life forms, cutting-edge air vehicle development, and insightful archaeological discoveries through ancient dental analysis.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant les avancées révolutionnaires dans le domaine des OLED à micro-échelle pour les lunettes intelligentes, la modélisation génomique avancée couvrant toutes les formes de vie, le développement de véhicules aériens de pointe et les découvertes archéologiques perspicaces grâce à l'analyse dentaire ancienne.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances en la innovación OLED a microescala para gafas inteligentes, modelos genómicos avanzados que abarcan todas las formas de vida, el desarrollo de vehículos aéreos de última generación y descubrimientos arqueológicos reveladores a través del análisis dental antiguo.

articles.title: World’s smallest OLED pixel could transform smart glasses
articles.summary: Researchers have built the smallest OLED pixel ever made—just 300 nanometers across—without sacrificing brightness. By redesigning the pixel with a nano-sized optical antenna and a protective insulation layer, they prevented the short circuits that normally plague devices at this scale. The result is a stable, ultra-tiny light source that could allow full HD displays to fit on an area the size of a grain of sand.
articles.content:
Smart glasses are widely seen as a breakthrough technology because they can project digital information directly into a person's line of sight. Yet real world adoption has lagged, largely because the hardware required to power these displays has been bulky and impractical. A major obstacle comes from classical optics, which suggests that shrinking efficient light emitting pixels down to the scale of the light's own wavelength should not work.

Physicists at Julius-Maximilians-Universität Würzburg (JMU) have now overcome that barrier. Using specially designed optical antennas, the team has built what they describe as the smallest pixel ever created. The research group, led by Professors Jens Pflaum and Bert Hecht, reported the advance in the journal Science Advances.

A Full HD Display on One Square Millimeter

"With the help of a metallic contact that allows current injection into an organic light-emitting diode while simultaneously amplifying and emitting the generated light, we have created a pixel for orange light on an area measuring just 300 by 300 nanometers. This pixel is just as bright as a conventional OLED pixel with normal dimensions of 5 by 5 micrometers," says Bert Hecht, describing the key finding of the study.

For scale, a nanometer is one millionth of a millimeter. At 300 by 300 nanometers, these pixels are extraordinarily small. In fact, a projector or display with a resolution of 1920 x 1080 pixels could fit within an area of just one square millimeter. Such compact dimensions could allow a display to be built directly into the arms of a pair of glasses, with the projected light directed onto the lenses.

OLED technology relies on multiple ultra thin organic layers positioned between two electrodes. When electricity passes through, electrons and holes recombine inside the active layer. This process excites the organic molecules, which then release energy as light quanta. Because each pixel produces its own light, no separate backlight is required. That design enables deep blacks, vibrant colors, and energy efficient performance for augmented and virtual reality (AR and VR) devices.

Why Shrinking OLED Pixels Is So Difficult

Simply scaling down existing OLED designs does not work at the nanoscale. The Würzburg team found that electrical current does not spread evenly when the structure becomes extremely small. "As with a lightning rod, simply reducing the size of the established OLED concept would cause the currents to emit mainly from the corners of the antenna," says Jens Pflaum, explaining the underlying physics. The gold antenna used in the device is shaped like a cuboid measuring 300 by 300 by 50 nanometers.

"The resulting electric fields would generate such strong forces that the gold atoms becoming mobile would gradually grow into the optically active material," Pflaum continues. These thread like growths, known as filaments, would keep extending until they created a short circuit and destroyed the pixel.

Insulation Layer Prevents Short Circuits

To solve this problem, the researchers introduced a precisely engineered insulating layer above the optical antenna. This layer leaves only a circular opening with a diameter of 200 nanometers at the center. By blocking current from flowing in at the edges and corners, the design ensures stable and reliable operation of the nano light-emitting diode. Under these conditions, filament formation is prevented. "Even the first nanopixels were stable for two weeks under ambient conditions," says Bert Hecht, describing the result.

The team's next goal is to boost efficiency beyond the current level of one percent and extend the color range to cover the full RGB spectrum. Achieving those milestones would clear the path for a new generation of miniature displays "made in Würzburg." In the future, displays and projectors based on this technology could become so compact that they are nearly invisible when integrated into wearable devices, from eyeglass frames to contact lenses.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in creating the world's smallest OLED pixel, enabling ultra-compact, high-resolution displays that could transform smart glasses and wearable devices. This advancement has broad potential impact on consumer electronics and augmented reality, offering a tangible and inspiring innovation with detailed context and clear benefits.
articles.personality_title: World’s smallest OLED pixel opens door to tiny, bright smart glasses displays
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Smart glasses can show digital images right in front of your eyes, but making the tiny screens inside them has been hard. The problem is that making light pixels very small usually causes them to stop working well.

**What happened** – Scientists at Julius-Maximilians-Universität Würzburg in Germany created the smallest OLED (organic light-emitting diode) pixel ever. It measures just 300 nanometers wide, which is about 300 times smaller than a human hair. They used a special tiny metal antenna and a new insulating layer to stop short circuits, allowing the pixel to shine as bright as bigger pixels.

**Impact** – This tiny pixel means a full high-definition screen could fit on an area the size of a grain of sand. That could let smart glasses have clear, colorful displays built right into their frames without being bulky. It also uses less energy and can show deep blacks, making images look better.

**What’s next step** – The researchers want to make these pixels even more efficient and add more colors, so the screens can show full-color pictures. If successful, this technology could be used in many wearable devices, maybe even contact lenses, making digital displays almost invisible.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have made the smallest bright OLED pixel that could shrink smart glasses displays down to tiny, practical sizes for everyday use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le plus petit pixel OLED au monde ouvre la voie à des écrans minuscules et lumineux pour lunettes intelligentes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les lunettes intelligentes peuvent afficher des images numériques directement devant vos yeux, mais fabriquer leurs écrans très petits est difficile. Le problème est que réduire la taille des pixels lumineux cause souvent des pannes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’université Julius-Maximilians de Würzburg en Allemagne ont créé le plus petit pixel OLED jamais fabriqué. Il mesure seulement 300 nanomètres de large, soit environ 300 fois plus petit qu’un cheveu humain. Ils ont utilisé une antenne métallique très petite et une couche isolante nouvelle pour éviter les courts-circuits, permettant au pixel de briller aussi fort que des pixels plus grands.

**Impact** – Ce pixel minuscule signifie qu’un écran haute définition complet pourrait tenir sur une surface aussi petite qu’un grain de sable. Cela pourrait permettre aux lunettes intelligentes d’avoir des écrans clairs et colorés intégrés dans leurs branches sans être encombrants. Cela consomme aussi moins d’énergie et permet d’avoir des noirs profonds, améliorant la qualité des images.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent rendre ces pixels encore plus efficaces et ajouter plus de couleurs pour que les écrans puissent afficher des images en couleur complète. Si cela réussit, cette technologie pourrait être utilisée dans de nombreux appareils portables, peut-être même des lentilles de contact, rendant les écrans numériques presque invisibles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont créé le plus petit pixel OLED lumineux qui pourrait réduire la taille des écrans de lunettes intelligentes à des dimensions pratiques pour un usage quotidien.
articles.personality_title_es: El píxel OLED más pequeño del mundo abre camino a pantallas diminutas y brillantes para gafas inteligentes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las gafas inteligentes pueden mostrar imágenes digitales justo frente a tus ojos, pero hacer las pantallas muy pequeñas ha sido difícil. El problema es que reducir el tamaño de los píxeles de luz suele causar fallos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Julius-Maximilians en Würzburg, Alemania, crearon el píxel OLED más pequeño que se ha hecho. Mide solo 300 nanómetros de ancho, unas 300 veces más pequeño que un cabello humano. Usaron una antena metálica muy pequeña y una capa aislante nueva para evitar cortocircuitos, permitiendo que el píxel brille tan fuerte como otros más grandes.

**Impacto** – Este píxel tan pequeño significa que una pantalla de alta definición completa podría caber en un área del tamaño de un grano de arena. Esto podría permitir que las gafas inteligentes tengan pantallas claras y coloridas integradas en sus patillas sin ser voluminosas. También consume menos energía y puede mostrar negros profundos, mejorando la calidad de las imágenes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren hacer estos píxeles aún más eficientes y agregar más colores para que las pantallas puedan mostrar imágenes a todo color. Si tienen éxito, esta tecnología podría usarse en muchos dispositivos portátiles, quizás incluso lentes de contacto, haciendo que las pantallas digitales sean casi invisibles.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los investigadores crearon el píxel OLED brillante más pequeño que podría reducir las pantallas de gafas inteligentes a tamaños prácticos para el uso diario.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a delicate pair of eyeglass frames with miniature glowing orange pixels embedded along the arms, each pixel depicted as tiny radiant cubes surrounded by a fine golden lattice resembling optical antennas, set against a soft, neutral background that highlights the intricate nanoscale technology and the promise of invisible, cutting-edge smart displays.

articles.title: Genome modelling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10176-5Evo 2 is an artificial intelligence-based biological foundation model trained on 9 trillion DNA base pairs spanning all domains of life that predicts functional properties from genomic sequences and provides a rich generative model for researchers in biology.
articles.content:
By learning the likelihood of sequences across vast evolutionary datasets, biological sequence models capture conserved sequence patterns that often reflect functional importance. These constraints allow the models to perform zero-shot prediction without any task-specific fine-tuning or supervision1,14,15,16. Here, likelihood refers to the probability that the model assigns to a given sequence, where mutations that reduce this probability are predicted to be deleterious. Given that Evo 2 learns a likelihood landscape across all three modalities of the central dogma (DNA, RNA and protein) and all three domains of life, we sought to assess whether Evo 2 could perform mutational effect prediction across these modalities and organisms (Fig. 2a).

Fig. 2: Evo 2 predicts mutational effects on protein, RNA and organismal fitness across all domains of life. a, Evo 2-predicted zero-shot likelihoods can be used to predict the effects of DNA, RNA or protein mutations on molecular function or organismal fitness. WT, wild type. b, Effects on Evo 2 prediction of sequence likelihood caused by mutations along gene start sites for various model species across the domains of life. See Extended Data Fig. 3a,b for additional analyses. T. kodakarensis, Thermococcus kodakarensis. c,d, For different prokaryotic (c) and eukaryotic (d) sequences, the likelihood of different types of mutations in different genomic elements were scored using Evo 2 7B. Scatter represents the median change in likelihood from wild type to mutant sequence per species, coloured by domain (c) or kingdom (d). Horizontal line indicates the median of the scatter distribution. lncRNA, long noncoding RNA; snRNA, small nuclear RNA. e, Mutational likelihoods were used to assess the ability of Evo 2 to differentiate between genomic sequences of model organisms on the basis of their usage of different stop codons. Shown are the standardized median of delta likelihood values across 5 species, where medians were calculated across approximately 4,100 randomly selected mutation loci. M. pneumoniae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae; P. tetraurelia, Paramecium tetraurelia. f, DMS assays were used to assess the Spearman correlation of zero-shot likelihoods from models with experimental assays. Notably, Evo 1 and GenSLM were exclusively trained on prokaryotic datasets. g, Schematic of our single-nucleotide resolution exon classifier based on embeddings from Evo 2. h, Single-nucleotide exon classifiers were trained on embeddings from Evo 2, Nucleotide Transformer (NT) and Evo 1, and were evaluated on the basis of their AUROC across eight held-out species. Performance was compared to SegmentNT-30 kb multispecies (asterisks indicate species in SegmentNT training data), ab initio AUGUSTUS, and to baseline nucleotide content and conservation metrics. D. rerio, Danio rerio; H. vulgare, Hordeum vulgare; S. moell., Selaginella moellendorffii; S. oleracea, Spinacia oleracea; T. cacao, Theobroma cacao; V. vinifera, Vitis vinifera. i, Genome browser track showing predictions from the Evo 2 embedding-based exon classifier scanned across the human STOML2 locus, where the vertical axis is the predicted classifier score and the horizontal axis is genome position. j, Evo 2 predicts genes as essential or nonessential, as determined by experimental gene essentiality assays across bacterial, archaeal and phage species (shown as overlaid scatter) using mutational likelihood of premature stop codon insertions (as a genetic perturbation). Full size image

To assess whether Evo 2 captures core biological principles, we first evaluated how single nucleotide variants (SNVs) affect Evo 2 likelihoods in the genomic sequences around the start codons of protein-coding genes. We introduced these mutations at each position in the wild-type sequence and calculated the resulting changes in Evo 2 predicted likelihoods across thousands of such loci (Fig. 2b and Extended Data Fig. 3a). We observed strong changes in the likelihood for mutations within the start codons in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. This was followed by a three-base periodicity pattern reflecting the triplet codons, with changes at the wobble positions showing lower impact on likelihood. For both prokaryotic and eukaryotic genomes, we observed a pattern upstream of the coding DNA sequence (CDS) that was consistent with the locations of known consensus sequences associated with translation initiation, namely, the Shine–Dalgarno sequence17 for prokaryotes and the Kozak sequence18 for eukaryotes. We also observed similar patterns for SNVs around stop codons (Extended Data Fig. 3b).

Next we measured the effect of mutations across a variety of both noncoding and coding sequences (Fig. 2c,d). Across 20 prokaryotic species and 16 eukaryotic species, we observed changes in model likelihoods consistent with known biological constraints. Non-synonymous mutations, premature stop codons and frameshift mutations caused much larger changes in likelihood than synonymous mutations. In noncoding regions, deletions in transfer RNAs (tRNAs) and ribosomal RNAs (rRNAs) had much larger effects than deletions in intergenic and other noncoding loci, reflecting the known essential roles of these RNAs. The 40B model exhibited higher sensitivity to deletions in microRNA (miRNA) and small nucleolar RNA (snoRNA) sequences compared with the 7B model. Evo 2 also predicted that less efficiently translated codons had lower likelihoods than more efficient codons (Extended Data Fig. 3c–e).

Recognizing that our training data contained genomes with distinct genetic codes, we tested how different premature stop codons impacted species that differ in their stop codon usage (Fig. 2e). We found that the model learned the difference between the standard code (stop codons TAA, TAG and TGA), the mycoplasma code (Code 4, stop codons TAA and TAG) and the ciliate code (Code 6, stop codon TGA). When ciliate genomes were artificially recoded to the standard genetic code, Evo 2 predicted mutations from the standard stop codons as deleterious, demonstrating that the model relies on sequence context to determine the appropriate genetic code (Extended Data Fig. 3f).

Although Evo 2 likelihoods reflect the expected importance of different genetic alterations, a key question is whether these likelihoods also correlate with functional effects, which can be empirically measured via deep mutational scanning (DMS) of proteins and noncoding RNAs (ncRNAs). Although state-of-the-art methods for this task tend to leverage both sequence alignments and structural conditioning, general-purpose single-sequence protein language models also learn likelihood distributions that correlate with fitness15. Evo 2 sequence likelihoods correlate with diverse definitions of fitness across nine prokaryotic protein datasets; six eukaryotic protein datasets; and seven datasets of rRNAs, tRNAs and ribozymes (Fig. 2f). Evo 2 is competitive with widely used ProGen language models for protein DMS and with RNA language models for ncRNA DMS, although it underperforms state-of-the-art models on protein DMS. Consistent with observed trends for protein language models, the performance of Evo 2 on these fitness prediction benchmarks begins to saturate and can decrease at the largest model scales19,20,21. We also tested the ability of Evo 2 to predict mutation effects in protein sequences from viruses that infect human hosts. We found no correlation between Evo 2 likelihood and viral protein fitness (Extended Data Fig. 2b), consistent with our data exclusions having the intended effect of weakening both language modelling and downstream performance (Extended Data Fig. 2a). Evo 2 likelihoods also have modest zero-shot association with human mRNA decay rates (Extended Data Fig. 3g and Supplementary Information B.2).

Since Evo 2 learns from eukaryotic genomes, which can be challenging to annotate, we assessed whether its embeddings capture exon–intron architecture. We trained lightweight models on Evo 2 7B base embeddings to develop single-nucleotide resolution classifiers of exon labels (Fig. 2g and Methods). On eight diverse species held out from classifier training, our best classifier achieved areas under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROCs) ranging from 0.91 to 0.99 (Fig. 2h,i), outperforming models trained on embeddings from other genomic language models, Nucleotide Transformer22 and Evo 1 (ref. 1), and classification by conservation metrics (local GC content and PhyloP). As a practical baseline, we show that our classifier outperforms ab initio AUGUSTUS23 across all species tested. Evo 2 also outperforms SegmentNT24 on all species outside the SegmentNT training set and on one of the three species in its training set. These results suggest that combining Evo 2 sequence embeddings with supervised approaches can aid the functional annotation of genetic components across diverse species, including non-model organisms.

Beyond molecular or gene-level prediction tasks, we previously showed that high likelihood under Evo 1 is associated with whole organism replication fitness in prokaryotes and phage as quantified by gene essentiality experiments1. Using zero-shot likelihoods to score the effects of premature stop codon insertions into bacterial, archaeal and phage genomes, we found that Evo 2 models performed similarly to Evo 1 and better than other zero-shot methods in predicting gene essentiality across diverse species (Fig. 2j and Extended Data Fig. 3h). On zero-shot prediction of human gene essentiality (Methods), Evo 2 40B (AUROC = 0.66, area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) = 0.15) outperformed other genomic language models (AUROC range 0.50–0.59, AUPRC range 0.09–0.12) and performs within the range of four PhyloP conservation scores (AUROC range 0.65–0.71, AUPRC range 0.13–0.21) (Extended Data Fig. 3i), although the overall predictive performance remains modest.

Together, these results demonstrate that Evo 2 captures information across biological modalities and domains of life. Notably, the 7B and 40B models expand predictive capabilities without compromising the prokaryotic insights captured by Evo 1. The utility of both zero-shot likelihoods and simple classifiers trained on Evo 2 embeddings for a variety of predictive tasks across prokaryotic and eukaryotic genomes indicates that Evo 2 provides a strong foundation model for downstream applications in computational biology.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Evo 2, an AI-based biological foundation model trained on an unprecedented scale of genomic data across all domains of life, which can predict functional properties from genomic sequences. This breakthrough has broad significance for biology and medicine, enabling improved understanding of genetic mutations, gene essentiality, and genome annotation across diverse species. The detailed description of Evo 2's capabilities and its validation across multiple datasets shows substantial substance and real-world impact, potentially accelerating research and applications in genomics and health.
articles.personality_title: Evo 2: AI model predicts gene functions across all life forms using vast DNA data
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study DNA to understand how genes work in different living things. DNA contains instructions for life, but predicting how changes in DNA affect organisms is very complex. New computer tools can help by learning from large amounts of genetic data.

**What happened** – Researchers created Evo 2, an artificial intelligence (AI) model trained on 9 trillion DNA base pairs from all types of life, including bacteria, plants, animals, and more. Evo 2 can predict how changes in DNA, RNA, or proteins will affect the function of genes and organisms. The model was tested on thousands of genetic sequences and could identify important parts of genes, predict harmful mutations, and help classify gene regions like exons (parts of genes that code for proteins).

**Impact** – Evo 2’s ability to learn from such a huge and diverse dataset makes it unique. It can recognize genetic rules across all life forms, including differences in how species use genetic codes. Evo 2 also predicts which genes are essential for survival in many organisms. This helps scientists understand how mutations might cause diseases or affect health, and can improve genome research in species that are not well studied.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use Evo 2 to speed up studies in genetics and medicine, such as identifying disease-causing mutations and improving gene editing. They may also combine Evo 2 with other tools to better annotate genomes of many species, helping research in agriculture, ecology, and health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Evo 2 is a powerful AI tool trained on vast genetic data that can predict gene functions and mutation effects across all forms of life, advancing biological research and medicine.
articles.personality_title_fr: Evo 2 : un modèle d'IA prédit les fonctions génétiques dans tous les êtres vivants grâce à d'énormes données ADN
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient l'ADN pour comprendre le fonctionnement des gènes chez différents organismes. L'ADN contient les instructions de la vie, mais prévoir comment les changements dans l'ADN affectent les êtres vivants est très complexe. De nouveaux outils informatiques peuvent aider en apprenant à partir de grandes quantités de données génétiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé Evo 2, un modèle d'intelligence artificielle (IA) entraîné sur 9 000 milliards de paires de bases d'ADN provenant de tous les types de vie, y compris bactéries, plantes, animaux, et plus encore. Evo 2 peut prédire comment les modifications de l'ADN, de l'ARN ou des protéines affectent la fonction des gènes et des organismes. Le modèle a été testé sur des milliers de séquences génétiques et a pu identifier des parties importantes des gènes, prédire les mutations nuisibles et aider à classer les régions génétiques comme les exons (parties codant pour des protéines).

**Impact** – La capacité d’Evo 2 à apprendre à partir d’un ensemble de données aussi vaste et diversifié le rend unique. Il reconnaît les règles génétiques dans tous les êtres vivants, y compris les différences d’utilisation des codes génétiques selon les espèces. Evo 2 prédit également quels gènes sont essentiels à la survie dans de nombreux organismes. Cela aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les mutations peuvent causer des maladies ou affecter la santé, et améliore la recherche sur le génome des espèces peu étudiées.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser Evo 2 pour accélérer les études en génétique et en médecine, comme l’identification des mutations responsables de maladies et l’amélioration de l’édition génétique. Ils pourraient aussi combiner Evo 2 avec d’autres outils pour mieux annoter les génomes de nombreuses espèces, aidant la recherche en agriculture, écologie et santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Evo 2 est un puissant outil d’IA entraîné sur d’immenses données génétiques qui peut prédire les fonctions génétiques et les effets des mutations dans tous les êtres vivants, faisant progresser la recherche en biologie et en médecine.
articles.personality_title_es: Evo 2: modelo de IA predice funciones genéticas en todas las formas de vida usando enorme cantidad de datos de ADN
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian el ADN para entender cómo funcionan los genes en diferentes seres vivos. El ADN contiene las instrucciones de la vida, pero predecir cómo los cambios en el ADN afectan a los organismos es muy complicado. Nuevas herramientas informáticas pueden ayudar aprendiendo de grandes cantidades de datos genéticos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon Evo 2, un modelo de inteligencia artificial (IA) entrenado con 9 billones de pares de bases de ADN de todos los tipos de vida, incluyendo bacterias, plantas, animales y más. Evo 2 puede predecir cómo los cambios en el ADN, ARN o proteínas afectan la función de los genes y organismos. El modelo fue probado en miles de secuencias genéticas y pudo identificar partes importantes de los genes, predecir mutaciones dañinas y ayudar a clasificar regiones genéticas como exones (partes que codifican proteínas).

**Impacto** – La capacidad de Evo 2 para aprender de un conjunto de datos tan grande y diverso lo hace único. Reconoce reglas genéticas en todas las formas de vida, incluyendo diferencias en el uso del código genético según la especie. Evo 2 también predice qué genes son esenciales para la supervivencia en muchos organismos. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo las mutaciones pueden causar enfermedades o afectar la salud, y mejora la investigación genómica en especies poco estudiadas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar Evo 2 para acelerar estudios en genética y medicina, como identificar mutaciones que causan enfermedades y mejorar la edición genética. También podrían combinar Evo 2 con otras herramientas para anotar mejor genomas de muchas especies, ayudando en agricultura, ecología y salud.

**Resumen en una frase** – Evo 2 es una poderosa herramienta de IA entrenada con enormes datos genéticos que puede predecir funciones genéticas y efectos de mutaciones en todas las formas de vida, impulsando la investigación biológica y médica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate, glowing double helix DNA strand seamlessly intertwining with elegant RNA ribbons and stylized protein structures, all floating above a softly illuminated globe that subtly blends representations of bacteria, archaea, and eukaryotic cells, symbolizing the unity of life’s domains through evolutionary sequence modeling.

articles.title: About Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP)
articles.summary: NASA’s Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP) studies, evaluates, and develops technologies and capabilities for new aircraft systems and explores far-future concepts for revolutionary air travel improvements. AAVP develops technologies for all flight regimes from hover to hypersonic to enable safe, new aircraft that are faster, quieter, and more fuel efficient. AAVP develops a broad range […]
articles.content:
NASA

NASA’s Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP) studies, evaluates, and develops technologies and capabilities for new aircraft systems and explores far-future concepts for revolutionary air travel improvements. AAVP develops technologies for all flight regimes from hover to hypersonic to enable safe, new aircraft that are faster, quieter, and more fuel efficient.

AAVP develops a broad range of technologies that maintain U.S. leadership in aerospace, benefitting the nation’s economy and quality of life. AAVP’s research primes the technology pipeline, bolstering U.S. competitiveness.

For subsonic transport aircraft, AAVP accelerates development of key technologies to ensure they will be ready by the late 2020s to transition into U.S. industry’s next-generation single-aisle transport aircraft. AAVP also explores high-risk, high-payoff concepts for future generations of aircraft. The program engages with partners from industry, academia, and other government agencies to maintain a broad perspective on technology solutions to aviation’s challenges, to pursue mutually beneficial collaborations, and to leverage opportunities for effective technology transition.

AAVP Projects

High Speed Flight (HSF)

Hi-Rate Composite Aircraft Manufacturing (HiCAM)

Subsonic Vehicles Technologies and Tools (SVTT)

Legacy AAVP Projects

Advanced Composites (ACP)

Advanced Air Transport Technology (AATT)

Commercial Supersonic Technology (CST)

Hybrid Thermally Efficient Core (HyTEC)

Hypersonic Technology (HT)

Revolutionary Vertical Lift Technology (RVLT)
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's Advanced Air Vehicles Program which is developing innovative aircraft technologies that promise safer, faster, quieter, and more fuel-efficient air travel. This has broad significance for the public by potentially transforming aviation, benefiting the economy, environment, and quality of life. The article provides sufficient detail about the program's scope, goals, and collaborative approach, making it a substantive positive technological advancement.
articles.personality_title: NASA advances new aircraft technologies for faster, quieter, and greener flights
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA runs the Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP) to develop new technologies for aircraft. The goal is to improve air travel by making planes safer, faster, quieter, and more fuel efficient.

**What happened** – AAVP researches and builds technologies that work for all kinds of flight, from hovering like a helicopter to flying at extreme speeds called hypersonic flight. The program works with companies, universities, and other government groups to create and test these new ideas. They focus on both current planes, like single-aisle jets used for many flights, and futuristic designs that could change air travel completely.

**Impact** – This program helps the U.S. stay a leader in making aircraft. By developing better materials, engines, and designs, AAVP aims to lower fuel use and noise pollution while increasing safety. This means future flights could be cheaper, quieter, and better for the environment. It also supports jobs and innovation in the aerospace industry.

**What's next step** – The technologies developed will be ready in the late 2020s to be used by aircraft manufacturers. AAVP will continue exploring bold ideas for future aircraft and work closely with partners to bring these new technologies into real planes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Advanced Air Vehicles Program is creating new aircraft technologies that promise safer, quieter, faster, and more fuel-efficient flights for the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA développe des technologies aériennes plus rapides, silencieuses et écologiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA dirige le programme Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP) pour développer de nouvelles technologies pour les avions. L’objectif est d’améliorer les voyages aériens en rendant les avions plus sûrs, plus rapides, plus silencieux et plus économes en carburant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’AAVP étudie et construit des technologies adaptées à tous les types de vol, du vol stationnaire comme un hélicoptère au vol hypersonique à très grande vitesse. Le programme collabore avec des entreprises, des universités et d’autres agences gouvernementales pour créer et tester ces nouvelles idées. Il se concentre à la fois sur les avions actuels, comme les avions monocouloirs, et sur des concepts futuristes qui pourraient transformer complètement le transport aérien.

**Impact** – Ce programme aide les États-Unis à rester leaders dans la fabrication d’avions. En développant de meilleurs matériaux, moteurs et designs, l’AAVP vise à réduire la consommation de carburant et le bruit tout en augmentant la sécurité. Cela signifie que les vols futurs pourraient être moins chers, plus silencieux et meilleurs pour l’environnement. Il soutient aussi l’emploi et l’innovation dans l’industrie aérospatiale.

**Prochaine étape** – Les technologies développées seront prêtes à la fin des années 2020 pour être utilisées par les fabricants d’avions. L’AAVP continuera d’explorer des idées audacieuses pour les avions du futur et de travailler étroitement avec ses partenaires pour intégrer ces nouvelles technologies dans des avions réels.

**Une phrase clé** – Le programme Advanced Air Vehicles de la NASA crée des technologies aériennes promettant des vols plus sûrs, plus silencieux, plus rapides et plus économes en carburant pour l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA desarrolla tecnologías aéreas más rápidas, silenciosas y ecológicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA dirige el Programa de Vehículos Aéreos Avanzados (AAVP) para crear nuevas tecnologías para aviones. El objetivo es mejorar los viajes aéreos haciendo los aviones más seguros, rápidos, silenciosos y que consuman menos combustible.

**Qué pasó** – El AAVP investiga y construye tecnologías que funcionan en todo tipo de vuelo, desde el vuelo estacionario como un helicóptero hasta el vuelo hipersónico a muy alta velocidad. El programa trabaja con empresas, universidades y otras agencias del gobierno para crear y probar estas nuevas ideas. Se enfoca tanto en aviones actuales, como los de un solo pasillo usados en muchos vuelos, como en diseños futuristas que podrían cambiar completamente el transporte aéreo.

**Impacto** – Este programa ayuda a que Estados Unidos siga siendo líder en la fabricación de aviones. Al desarrollar mejores materiales, motores y diseños, el AAVP busca reducir el uso de combustible y la contaminación sonora mientras mejora la seguridad. Esto significa que los vuelos del futuro podrían ser más baratos, silenciosos y mejores para el medio ambiente. También apoya los empleos y la innovación en la industria aeroespacial.

**Próximo paso** – Las tecnologías desarrolladas estarán listas a finales de la década de 2020 para ser usadas por los fabricantes de aviones. El AAVP continuará explorando ideas audaces para futuros aviones y trabajará de cerca con sus socios para llevar estas tecnologías a aviones reales.

**Una frase clave** – El Programa de Vehículos Aéreos Avanzados de la NASA está creando tecnologías que prometen vuelos más seguros, silenciosos, rápidos y eficientes para el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sleek, futuristic aircraft soaring above softly rolling hills under a calm sky, its form blending elements of both a quiet subsonic plane and a dynamic hypersonic vehicle, symbolizing advanced air travel innovation and technological progress, with subtle abstract patterns resembling interconnected gears and circuits beneath the aircraft to represent cutting-edge aerospace research and collaboration.

articles.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
articles.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.
articles.content:
Teeth can hold remarkable clues about how people lived thousands of years ago. A new study published in the open access journal PLOS One, led by Roberto Germano of Sapienza University of Rome and colleagues, shows how the teeth of Iron Age Italians preserve detailed evidence about their health, diet, and early life experiences.

Understanding daily life in ancient cultures depends on reliable biological records. Teeth are especially valuable because they are extremely durable and retain microscopic signs of growth and change. By combining several types of dental analysis, the researchers examined individuals buried at Pontecagnano, an Iron Age site in southern Italy dating to the 7th and 6th centuries BC.

Childhood Stress Recorded in Dental Growth

The team studied growth patterns in the dental tissue of 30 teeth from 10 individuals. By comparing canine and molar teeth, they reconstructed patterns of development during the first six years of life. The enamel showed subtle disruptions around one year and again around four years of age. These stress signals likely reflect sensitive stages of early childhood, when shifts in diet and behavior can increase vulnerability to illness.

Iron Age Diet Revealed in Dental Plaque

The researchers also analyzed dental plaque, known as calculus, to uncover what these individuals ate as adults. Inside the hardened plaque, they found microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, plant fibers, and yeast spores. The findings point to a carbohydrate rich diet and provide strong evidence that fermented foods and beverages were regularly consumed.

These results are consistent with earlier research suggesting that contact with Mediterranean cultures expanded available food resources during this period. The evidence supports the idea that Iron Age communities in this region were adapting to changing environmental and social conditions.

First Histological Data From Pontecagnano

This research offers the first histological data from the Iron Age community of Pontecagnano. It also demonstrates how combining multiple dental techniques can generate detailed reconstructions of individual life histories. However, because the study included only 10 individuals, the findings should not be viewed as representative of the entire population. Instead, they provide in depth insights into specific lives.

The authors note that future research using larger sample sizes and additional tools such as isotopic analysis could significantly deepen our understanding of ancient communities.

Roberto Germano adds: "The teeth of Pontecagnano's Iron Age inhabitants opened a unique window onto their lives: we could follow childhood growth and health with remarkable precision and identify traces of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods in adulthood, revealing how this community adapted to environmental and social challenges."

Alessia Nava adds: "The study of the histomorphometry of deciduous and permanent teeth from individuals found in ancient necropolises makes it possible to go beyond the narrow focus on the period close to their death and brings to the forefront the life of each of them during their early years. This and other modern approaches represent a major technological and disciplinary advancement that is revolutionizing the study of the biocultural adaptations of past populations."

Emanuela Cristiani adds: "In the case of Pontecagnano, the analysis of dental calculus revealed starch granules from cereals and legumes, yeast spores, and plant fibers, providing a very concrete picture of the diet and some daily activities of these Iron Age communities, and offering strong evidence of the regular consumption of fermented foods and beverages."

Funding: Carmen Esposito (CE) was supported by the European Union's Horizon Europe programme under the Marie Skłodowska Curie Actions Postdoctoral Fellowship, Grant Agreement No. 101065320 (TULAR). Dental calculus analyses were carried out in a laboratory funded by the European Research Council (ERC) Starting Grant HIDDEN FOODS, Grant Agreement No. 639286 (PI: Emanuela Cristiani, EC). Alessia Nava (AN) received funding from the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union's Horizon Europe program, Grant Agreement No. 101077348 (MOTHERS). Roberto Germano (RG) received support from the Environmental Biology Doctoral School, Sapienza University of Rome. The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study using advanced dental analysis techniques to uncover detailed insights into the health, diet, and childhood stress of Iron Age Italians. This research provides meaningful contributions to archaeology and anthropology, revealing how ancient communities adapted to environmental and social challenges. The findings have broad significance for understanding human history and biocultural adaptation, supported by substantial context and detail.
articles.personality_title: Ancient teeth reveal childhood and diet of Iron Age Italians
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study teeth from people who lived in southern Italy about 2,700 years ago during the Iron Age. Teeth are very strong and can keep tiny clues about health and food from long ago.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Roberto Germano analyzed 30 teeth from 10 individuals found at an ancient site called Pontecagnano. They looked at growth lines in the enamel to find signs of stress during early childhood. They also studied hardened plaque on the teeth to identify tiny remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods that these people ate.

**Impact** – This study gives a detailed look at how these ancient people grew and what they ate. The growth lines show that children faced health challenges around ages one and four. The plaque shows a diet rich in carbohydrates and regular use of fermented foods, suggesting connections with other Mediterranean cultures. This helps us understand how people adapted to their environment and social changes long ago.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to study more teeth and use other methods like isotope analysis to learn even more about ancient diets and lifestyles. This could give a clearer picture of the whole population and how communities changed over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By studying ancient teeth, scientists uncovered detailed stories of childhood stress and diverse diets among Iron Age Italians, showing how they lived nearly 3,000 years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des dents anciennes révèlent l'enfance et l'alimentation des Italiens de l'âge du fer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques étudient des dents de personnes ayant vécu il y a environ 2 700 ans dans le sud de l'Italie, à l'âge du fer. Les dents sont très solides et peuvent garder de petits indices sur la santé et l'alimentation d'autrefois.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Roberto Germano a analysé 30 dents appartenant à 10 individus découverts sur le site ancien de Pontecagnano. Ils ont examiné les lignes de croissance dans l'émail pour détecter des signes de stress pendant la petite enfance. Ils ont aussi étudié le tartre durci sur les dents pour identifier des restes microscopiques de céréales, légumineuses et aliments fermentés consommés par ces personnes.

**Impact** – Cette étude offre un aperçu précis de la croissance et de l'alimentation de ces anciens habitants. Les lignes de croissance montrent que les enfants ont connu des difficultés de santé vers un et quatre ans. Le tartre révèle un régime riche en glucides et la consommation régulière d’aliments fermentés, suggérant des liens avec d’autres cultures méditerranéennes. Cela aide à comprendre comment ils s’adaptaient à leur environnement et aux changements sociaux.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs souhaitent étudier plus de dents et utiliser d’autres méthodes comme l’analyse isotopique pour mieux comprendre les régimes alimentaires et modes de vie anciens. Cela pourrait donner une image plus complète de la population et de son évolution.

**Phrase clé** – En étudiant des dents anciennes, les scientifiques ont découvert des histoires détaillées sur le stress infantile et la diversité alimentaire des Italiens de l'âge du fer, révélant leur mode de vie il y a près de 3 000 ans.
articles.personality_title_es: Dientes antiguos revelan la infancia y dieta de los italianos de la Edad de Hierro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos estudian dientes de personas que vivieron hace unos 2,700 años en el sur de Italia, durante la Edad de Hierro. Los dientes son muy resistentes y pueden conservar pequeñas pistas sobre la salud y la alimentación de entonces.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Roberto Germano analizó 30 dientes de 10 individuos encontrados en un sitio antiguo llamado Pontecagnano. Examinaron las líneas de crecimiento en el esmalte para encontrar señales de estrés en la infancia. También estudiaron la placa dental endurecida para identificar restos microscópicos de cereales, legumbres y alimentos fermentados que estas personas consumían.

**Impacto** – Este estudio ofrece una visión detallada de cómo crecían estas personas antiguas y qué comían. Las líneas de crecimiento muestran que los niños enfrentaron problemas de salud alrededor de los 1 y 4 años. La placa revela una dieta rica en carbohidratos y el consumo regular de alimentos fermentados, lo que sugiere conexiones con otras culturas mediterráneas. Esto ayuda a entender cómo se adaptaban a su entorno y a los cambios sociales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan estudiar más dientes y usar otros métodos, como el análisis isotópico, para aprender aún más sobre las dietas y estilos de vida antiguos. Esto podría ofrecer una imagen más clara de toda la población y de cómo cambiaron las comunidades con el tiempo.

**Frase clave** – Al estudiar dientes antiguos, los científicos descubrieron historias detalladas sobre el estrés infantil y dietas diversas entre los italianos de la Edad de Hierro, mostrando cómo vivían hace casi 3,000 años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of ancient Iron Age teeth partially embedded in softly glowing earth, surrounded by delicate microscopic depictions of cereal grains, legumes, plant fibers, and yeast spores floating gently around them, symbolizing childhood growth and diet in a southern Italian archaeological setting, rendered in natural, muted earth tones.
